item.title: American
item.description: Layered fat with restrained sweetness in bakes and bright finishes (lemon, mustard, vinegar) in savory dishes. The throughline is dialing back what factory food overdoes.

item.title: Chinese
item.description: A four-axis pantry (soy, vinegar, aromatic, heat) combined in shifting ratios across regions. Every dish reads the cook's hand on those four levers.

item.title: Italian
item.description: Restraint plus fat: olive oil, cured pork, aged cheese, acid. The technique is in the ratios, not the ingredient count.

item.title: Thai
item.description: Balance across four registers (sweet, salt, heat, sour) held within a single dish, not separated across courses. The Thai catalog here is drinks, for now, and they teach extraction.

item.title: Digital Scale
item.description: Weighs ingredients in grams. The single most consequential tool for repeatable baking and accurate recipe scaling.

item.title: Dutch Oven
item.description: 5–7 quart enameled cast iron pot with a heavy lid. Mass for steady braising, seal for moisture, depth for stocks — and stovetop-to-oven without a transfer.

item.title: Heavy Saucepan
item.description: 2–3 quart pan with a thick conductive base. Thermal mass is the feature: stable low heat for tea, custards, and reductions that won't scorch.

item.title: Instant-Read Thermometer
item.description: Probe thermometer that reads internal temperature in 1–3 seconds. Replaces guesswork with a number on protein doneness, custards, brown butter, and oil.

item.title: Microplane
item.description: Photo-etched fine grater. Turns hard cheese, citrus zest, garlic, and ginger into a fluffy, dissolvable powder.

item.title: Wide Skillet
item.description: 12-inch sauté pan with sloped sides. The width is the spec: surface area drives evaporation, browning, and the room you need to finish pasta in the pan.

item.title: Wok
item.description: High-walled carbon steel pan for stir-frying. The shape concentrates heat at the base and routes ingredients up the cooler walls between bursts of contact.

item.title: Cook
item.description: Tested recipes. Filter by cuisine, course, diet, time, and difficulty.

item.title: Explore
item.description: Four families gather every entry. Pick a family for a focused walk, or jump straight to a category.

item.title: Learn
item.description: Techniques that show up across many recipes — what they are, when they matter, how they fail.

item.title: Pantry
item.description: What ingredients are, how to choose and store them, what to swap. Plus the equipment that earns its drawer.

item.title: Traverse
item.description: Cross-cutting indices into the library — by region, by curated path, by attribute.

item.title: Chinese staples
item.description: The dishes that teach the Chinese pantry: the light/dark soy distinction, Chinkiang acid, Sichuan má-là, and sesame-paste body.

item.title: Holiday table
item.description: A curated meal — protein, sides, dessert — that holds together as one table rather than four good dishes that happen to share a date.

item.title: Tea drinks
item.description: Extraction is the technique. Each of these gets the leaf right before it gets anything else right.

item.title: Weekend bake
item.description: Bakes that reward weekend time: brown butter, properly creamed butter and sugar, cocoa bloomed before it hits the batter.

item.title: Weeknight pasta
item.description: Pasta dishes that hit the table in under 30 minutes without sacrificing technique.

item.title: All-purpose flour
item.description: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the road — strong enough for bread in a pinch, soft enough for cake if you handle it gently.

item.title: Assam tea
item.description: Robust, malty black tea from northeast India. The default base for masala chai and what gives English Breakfast its backbone.

item.title: Avocado oil
item.description: Pressed from avocado flesh, mostly monounsaturated fat. Holds high heat without smoking and stays neutral enough for searing where olive oil's fruit notes would intrude.

item.title: Baking powder
item.description: A pre-mixed chemical leavener: baking soda plus one or two dry acids plus a starch buffer. Double-acting versions release CO2 in two stages — once when wet, again at oven heat.

item.title: Baking soda
item.description: Pure sodium bicarbonate. An alkali that needs an acid in the batter to release CO2 and lift — and that simultaneously raises pH, deepening browning.

item.title: Black peppercorns
item.description: Dried unripe fruit of Piper nigrum. Heat from piperine; aroma from terpenes that vanish on grinding.

item.title: Butter (unsalted)
item.description: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever in cooking.

item.title: Chili oil (辣椒油)
item.description: Neutral oil infused with dried chilies and aromatics, with the toasted chili sediment retained. Both the clear oil and the sediment carry distinct functions in a dish.

item.title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
item.description: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic — closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.
